title: The connection between hair loss and sustained cortisol elevation
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Your hair is not betraying you. It's trying to tell you something. 

 I started losing my hair in my early thirties. Not in a dignified, slow receding kin...
htmlContent:
Your hair is not betraying you. It's trying to tell you something.

I started losing my hair in my early thirties. Not in a dignified, slow receding kind of way. In clumps. In the shower drain, on my pillow, across my desk chair. I'd run my hand through it and come back with strands wrapped around my fingers like tiny accusations. I went to dermatologists. I tried topical treatments. I spent money on shampoos that promised to fix everything. Nothing worked. And nobody asked me about my stress levels. Not once.

Here's the thing. Hair loss is not just genetic. It's not just about DHT or thyroid function or whatever supplement company is trying to sell you this month. It's also about your nervous system. Specifically, it's about what happens when your body stays in a state of threat for too long. When your cortisol stays elevated for months or years at a time. When your body decides that growing hair is not a survival priority. And it makes that decision without asking you.

The cortisol-hair connection that nobody talks about

Let me be clear about something. I'm not saying stress causes all hair loss. That's not true and I won't pretend it is. But for a lot of us, especially those of us with complex trauma histories, sustained cortisol elevation plays a major role in thinning hair, shedding, and even conditions like telogen effluvium and alopecia areata. The research is solid on this.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, has talked extensively about how the nervous system prioritizes survival over non-essential functions. Hair growth is non-essential. Your body doesn't care about your hair when it thinks a tiger is chasing you. The problem is that for trauma survivors, the tiger never actually leaves. Your nervous system stays in that high-alert state, pumping out cortisol day after day, and your hair follicles take the hit.

Think about it. Cortisol is a glucocorticoid. It's designed to give you quick energy in an emergency. But when it's chronically elevated, it starts to break things down. It can push hair follicles into a resting phase called telogen. Normally, about 10 percent of your hair is in this phase at any given time. But with sustained cortisol, that number can jump to 30, 40, even 50 percent. And then you start shedding. Wild, right?

I remember sitting in a doctor's office, holding a lab report that showed my cortisol levels were through the roof. The doctor said "you're stressed" like it was a personality flaw. Like I could just decide to stop. Like my nervous system was making a choice. It wasn't. It was responding to decades of accumulated threat. And my hair was just the most visible sign of something much deeper.

What happens inside your body when cortisol stays high

Let's get specific about the mechanism here, because understanding it matters. Your hair follicles have cortisol receptors. They're directly wired to respond to stress hormones. When cortisol binds to these receptors, it can actually shorten the growth phase of your hair. It can also increase inflammation around the follicle. And inflammation is not your friend keeping hair on your head.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma gets stored in the body. He talks about how unresolved survival responses create a kind of stuck energy that keeps the nervous system locked in threat mode. That stuck energy is not a metaphor. It's a physiological reality. And it shows up in your cortisol levels, which show up in your hair.

I want to be honest with you about something that took me years to accept. No amount of expensive shampoo or topical treatments will fix a dysregulated nervous system. You can apply all the serums in the world to your scalp, but if your body is still pumping out cortisol like it's trying to outrun a predator, your hair is going to keep falling out. That's not a marketing problem. That's a biology problem.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Levine walks through how the body holds trauma and how we can gently release it. It's not a quick fix. But it's real.

The difference between normal shedding and trauma-related hair loss

Everybody loses some hair. It's normal to shed 50 to 100 strands a day. But there's a difference between normal shedding and the kind of loss that comes from a dysregulated nervous system. Here's what I noticed in myself and what I've heard from countless others in the trauma recovery community.

Normal shedding is consistent. Trauma-related loss comes in waves. You might go through a period where you're losing a lot, then it stabilizes, then another wave hits. It often coincides with major life stressors, but sometimes it doesn't. Sometimes it just shows up because your body finally hit its limit. And the loss tends to be diffuse, meaning it's all over your head, not just at the temples or crown.

Stay with me here, because this part matters. When your cortisol is chronically elevated, your body also produces less of something called insulin-like growth factor 1, or IGF-1. This is a hormone that helps regulate hair growth. So you've got too much of the thing that shuts down hair growth and not enough of the thing that supports it. It's a double hit. And your hair pays the price.

I started tracking my shedding patterns alongside my nervous system state. I noticed that after a particularly rough week of flashbacks or hypervigilance, my hair would start shedding more about two to three months later. That's the delay. That's how long it takes for the cortisol to work its way through the follicle cycle. So when you're losing hair now, it's often a reflection of what your nervous system was doing months ago. Not what's happening right now. That's frustrating, but it also means that if you start regulating your nervous system today, you won't see the benefit in your hair for a while. But the benefit is coming.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana, who studied under Porges, breaks down how to actually apply polyvagal theory to daily life. It's not abstract. It's practical.

What actually helps, from a nervous system perspective

I'm going to tell you what helped me, but I need you to hear something first. This is not a protocol. This is not a five-step plan to regrow your hair. I don't believe in those. What I believe in is addressing the root cause, and the root cause for many of us is a nervous system that doesn't know how to come back down.

First, I had to stop fighting my hair loss. The more I panicked about it, the more cortisol I produced, and the worse it got. It became a feedback loop. I was literally stressing myself out about being stressed out. That's the trap. And the only way out is to stop making your hair the problem and start making your nervous system the focus.

Second, I started doing very gentle, very slow somatic practices. Not intense workouts. Not pushing myself. Just lying on the floor with my feet up, doing what Levine calls pendulation. Moving between a sensation of safety and a sensation of activation, slowly, over and over. It felt stupid at first. It felt like I was doing nothing. But over months, my cortisol started to drop. And my hair started to come back.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). I use it on my upper back and shoulders, where I hold most of my tension. It's not a cure. But it helps signal to your body that it's safe to let go.

Third, I had to address my sleep. Cortisol and sleep have a bidirectional relationship. High cortisol disrupts sleep, and poor sleep keeps cortisol high. For me, this meant creating a sleep environment that felt genuinely safe. Not just dark and quiet, but safe in a nervous system sense. I needed weight on my body. I needed to feel held.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). It's not magic. But for some of us, the deep pressure stimulation helps drop us into a more regulated state. And that matters for hair growth, because hair grows best when your body is in rest and digest mode, not fight or flight.

The hard truth about recovery

I want to be honest about something. My hair never fully returned to what it was before. Some of the loss was permanent. And I've had to make peace with that. But the shedding stopped. The diffuse thinning reversed. And more , I stopped living in a state of constant threat. That's the real win.

Here's what I wish someone had told me when I was Googling hair loss remedies at 2 AM, crying into my pillow. Your hair is not the enemy. It's not betraying you. It's just a symptom of a deeper dysregulation. And when you address that dysregulation, your body will start to heal in ways you can't predict. Maybe your hair comes back fully. Maybe it doesn't. But you'll feel different. You'll feel more alive. You'll feel less like a victim of your own biology.

Bessel van der Kolk said something that stuck with me. He said the body keeps the score. And that's true. Your hair is keeping the score. But it's also capable of changing the score. Your nervous system is plastic. It can learn new patterns. It can learn safety. It can learn to come back down.

I'm not going to tell you that if you just relax, your hair will grow back. That's toxic positivity and I won't do it. What I will tell you is that your nervous system matters. It matters for everything. For your sleep, your digestion, your mood, your relationships, and yes, your hair. When you start taking care of it, everything shifts. Not perfectly. Not quickly. But genuinely.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your shoulders drop a little? Did you take a breath? That's your nervous system responding. That's the beginning of change.
